import json
import re
from collections import Counter

# Read the data from the file
file_path = var_functions.query_db:16
with open(file_path, 'r', encoding='utf-8') as f:
    businesses = json.load(f)

# Extract categories from descriptions and count them
business_data = []
category_count = Counter()

for business in businesses:
    business_id = business['business_id']
    description = business.get('description', '')
    
    # Extract the numeric ID and convert to business_ref format
    # businessid_X -> businessref_X
    if 'businessid_' in business_id:
        ref_id = business_id.replace('businessid_', 'businessref_')
    else:
        ref_id = business_id
    
    # Extract categories from description
    # Look for text after "services in", "offers", "including", etc.
    categories = []
    desc_lower = description.lower()
    
    # Try to find category sections in description
    if 'services in' in desc_lower:
        # Extract text after "services in"
        match = re.search(r'services in ([^.]+)', description, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            cats_text = match.group(1)
            # Split by commas and clean up
            cats = [c.strip() for c in cats_text.split(',')]
            categories.extend(cats)
    
    if 'including' in desc_lower:
        # Extract text after "including"
        match = re.search(r'including ([^.]+)', description, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            cats_text = match.group(1)
            # Split by commas and clean up
            cats = [c.strip() for c in cats_text.split(',')]
            categories.extend(cats)
    
    # Also look for "range of services" patterns
    if 'range of services' in desc_lower:
        patterns = [
            r'range of services,? including ([^.]+)',
            r'range of services in ([^.]+)',
            r'offers a range of ([^.]+)',
            r'offers a ([^.]+) range'
        ]
        for pattern in patterns:
            match = re.search(pattern, description, re.IGNORECASE)
            if match:

    business_id = business['business_id']

    # Extract the numeric ID and convert to business_ref format
    # businessid_X -> businessref_X
    if 'businessid_' in business_id:
        ref_id = business_id.replace('businessid_', 'businessref_')
    else:
        ref_id = business_id
    
    # Extract categories from description
    # Look for text after "services in", "offers", "including", etc.
    categories = []
    desc_lower = description.lower()
    
    # Try to find category sections in description
    if 'services in' in desc_lower:
        # Extract text after "services in"
        match = re.search(r'services in ([^.]+)', description, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            cats_text = match.group(1)
            # Split by commas and clean up
            cats = [c.strip() for c in cats_text.split(',')]
            categories.extend(cats)
    
    if 'including' in desc_lower:
        # Extract text after "including"
        match = re.search(r'including ([^.]+)', description, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            cats_text = match.group(1)
            # Split by commas and clean up
            cats = [c.strip() for c in cats_text.split(',')]
            categories.extend(cats)
    
    # Also look for "range of services" patterns
    if 'range of services' in desc_lower:
        patterns = [
            r'range of services,? including ([^.]+)',
            r'range of services in ([^.]+)',
            r'offers a range of ([^.]+)',
            r'offers a ([^.]+) range'
        ]
        for pattern in patterns:
            match = re.search(pattern, description, re.IGNORECASE)
            if match:
                cats_text = match.group(1)
                # Split by commas and clean up

                categories.extend(cats)
                break
    
    # Clean up categories
    cleaned_categories = []
    for cat in categories:
        # Remove trailing periods and extra whitespace
        cat = cat.strip().rstrip('.')
        # Remove "and" from the beginning/end if present
        if cat.startswith('and '):
            cat = cat[4:]
        if cat.endswith(' and'):
            cat = cat[:-4]
        if cat:
            cleaned_categories.append(cat)
    
    # If no categories found, use common patterns from the description
    if not cleaned_categories:
        # Check for specific known categories in the text
        known_categories = [
            'Hair Salons', 'Beauty & Spas', 'Nail Salons', 'Restaurants',
            'Food', 'Shopping', 'Health & Medical', 'Bars', 'Nightlife',
            'Active Life', 'Automotive', 'Home Services', 'Local Services',
            'Education', 'Preschools', 'Grocery', 'Hotels & Travel',
            'Fitness & Instruction', 'Pets', 'Professional Services'
        ]
        
        for known_cat in known_categories:
            if known_cat.lower() in desc_lower:
                cleaned_categories.append(known_cat)
    
    # Add each category to the counter
    for cat in cleaned_categories:
        category_count[cat] += 1
        
    business_data.append({
        'business_id': business_id,
        'ref_id': ref_id,
        'categories': cleaned_categories
    })

# Print summary
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'total_businesses': len(businesses),
    'categories_found': len(category_count),

    'category_count': dict(category_count)
}))

val_args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import Counter

# Access the stored result
file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:16']

# Read the data from the file
with open(file_path, 'r', encoding='utf-8') as f:
    businesses = json.load(f)

# Extract categories from descriptions and count them
category_count = Counter()

for business in businesses:
    description = business.get('description', '')
    
    # Extract categories from description
    categories = []
    desc_lower = description.lower()
    
    # Try different patterns to extract categories
    patterns = [
        r'services in ([^.]+)',
        r'including ([^.]+)',
        r'offers ([^.]+) range',
        r'range of services,? including ([^.]+)',
        r'range of services in ([^.]+)'
    ]
    
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, desc_lower)
        if match:
            cats_text = match.group(1)
            # Split by commas and clean up
            cats = [c.strip() for c in cats_text.split(',')]
            categories.extend(cats)
            break
    
    # If no categories found, look for known category patterns
    if not categories:
        # Check for specific phrases that indicate categories
        if 'hair salon' in desc_lower:
            categories.append('Hair Salons')
        if 'beauty' in desc_lower or 'spa' in desc_lower:
            categories.append('Beauty & Spas')
        if 'nail salon' in desc_lower:
            categories.append('Nail Salons')
        if 'restaurant' in desc_lower:
            categories.append('Restaurants')
        if 'food' in desc_lower:
            categories.append('Food')
        if 'shopping' in desc_lower or 'store' in desc_lower:
            categories.append('Shopping')
        if 'health' in desc_lower or 'medical' in desc_lower:
            categories.append('Health & Medical')
        if 'bar' in desc_lower or 'nightlife' in desc_lower:
            categories.append('Bars')
        if 'automotive' in desc_lower or 'auto' in desc_lower:
            categories.append('Automotive')
        if 'home services' in desc_lower:
            categories.append('Home Services')
        if 'education' in desc_lower or 'school' in desc_lower:
            categories.append('Education')
    
    # Clean up categories
    cleaned_categories = []
    for cat in categories:
        cat = cat.strip().rstrip('.')
        if cat.startswith('and '):
            cat = cat[4:]
        if cat:
            cleaned_categories.append(cat)
    
    # Add each category to the counter
    for cat in cleaned_categories:
        category_count[cat] += 1

# Get the most common category
most_common = category_count.most_common(1)
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'total_businesses': len(businesses),
    'categories_found': len(category_count),
    'most_common_category': most_common[0] if most_common else None,
    'all_categories': dict(category_count.most_common(20))
}))

var_functions.query_db:5.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

var_functions.query_db:5.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!
